content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:1faac195

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: force_memory_write=true and transcript contains durable user/family context plus reusable trip-planning preferences and a richer planning thread.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements need the stable namespace-level USER entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=my family; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's family; reason=A reusable referent for claims about the user's planned family road trip; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=my sister Emily; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PERSON; name=Emily; reason=Named person with explicit relationship to the user; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=Denver; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=LOCATION; name=Denver; reason=Concrete location repeatedly discussed as the destination and venue city.
- mention=my niece Emma; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PERSON; name=Emma; reason=Named person with explicit relationship to the user; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=Denver Philharmonic Orchestra / DPO; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Denver Philharmonic Orchestra; reason=Concrete organization selected by the user for possible family concerts.
- mention=Family Concerts series; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Denver Philharmonic Orchestra Family Concerts series; reason=Reusable named series that later claims may target; scoped to DPO to avoid ambiguity.
- mention=Musical Storytime concert; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=Musical Storytime; reason=Specific selected event likely to be the object of later claims.
- mention=McNichols Civic Center Building; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=LOCATION; name=McNichols Civic Center Building; reason=Concrete venue where the selected concert is described as being held.
- mention=Colorado Symphony / CSO; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Colorado Symphony; reason=Named organization suggested as a possible kid-friendly performance provider.
- mention=Denver Center for the Performing Arts / DCPA; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Denver Center for the Performing Arts; reason=Named organization/venue suggested for kid-friendly productions.
- mention=Boettcher Concert Hall; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=LOCATION; name=Boettcher Concert Hall; reason=Concrete venue named for suggested performances.
- mention=Denver Young Artists Orchestra; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Denver Young Artists Orchestra; reason=Named organization suggested as a possible performance option.
- mention=Swallow Hill Music Hall; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=LOCATION; name=Swallow Hill Music Hall; reason=Named venue suggested for family-friendly music performances.
- mention=Denver Children's Theatre; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Denver Children's Theatre; reason=Named theatre company suggested for child-oriented performances.
- mention=The Denver Central Market; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=LOCATION; name=The Denver Central Market; reason=Named lunch option near the venue, likely reusable for claims about kid-friendly amenities.
- mention=The Kitchen Denver; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=LOCATION; name=The Kitchen Denver; reason=Named lunch option near the venue.
- mention=Modern Market; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<17>; type=LOCATION; name=Modern Market; reason=Named lunch option; canonical identity is somewhat ambiguous because the chain/location is not fully specified.
- mention=The Delectable Egg; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<18>; type=LOCATION; name=The Delectable Egg; reason=Named lunch/brunch option near the venue.
- mention=My Kid's Eye View; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<19>; type=LOCATION; name=My Kid's Eye View; reason=Named play cafe suggested as a kid-friendly lunch option.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<20>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=User|Emily|Emma|Denver|McNichols Civic Center Building|Denver Philharmonic Orchestra|Musical Storytime
        text_queries=sister Emily in Denver|niece Emma piano lessons|Denver family trip kid-friendly attractions Musical Storytime McNichols|kid-friendly lunch near McNichols Civic Center Building
        predicate_hints=has_goal|current_location|has_experience_with|prefers|attended_event

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/20 (Sat) 10:41
Transcript:
user: I'm planning a road trip with my family and need some recommendations for kid-friendly attractions in different cities. Do you have any suggestions?
assistant: What a wonderful adventure you're planning! I'd be delighted to help you with kid-friendly attractions in various cities. Here are some popular and fun suggestions:

**East Coast:**

1. **Orlando, Florida**:
	* Walt Disney World (Magic Kingdom, Epcot, Hollywood Studios, and Animal Kingdom)
	* Universal Orlando Resort (Universal Studios, Islands of Adventure, and Volcano Bay)
	* LEGOLAND Florida
2. **Washington, D.C.**:
	* National Zoo (free admission)
	* National Museum of Natural History (hands-on exhibits and dinosaur fossils)
	* National Children's Museum (play-based learning)
3. **New York City, New York**:
	* American Museum of Natural History (dinosaurs, space, and more)
	* Central Park Zoo and Conservatory Garden
	* Children's Museum of the Arts (interactive art exhibits)

**Midwest:**

1. **Chicago, Illinois**:
	* Navy Pier (amusement park, boat cruises, and entertainment)
	* Lincoln Park Zoo (free admission)
	* Museum of Science and Industry (hands-on exhibits and a coal mine tour)
2. **Indianapolis, Indiana**:
	* Indianapolis Zoo (African Plains exhibit and Dolphin Show)
	* Children's Museum of Indianapolis (largest children's museum in the world)
	* Conner Prairie (interactive historical areas and farm)

**South:**

1. **Gatlinburg, Tennessee**:
	* Dollywood (Dolly Parton's theme park with roller coasters and live shows)
	* Ripley's Aquarium of the Smokies (shark tunnel and penguin exhibit)
	* Ober Gatlinburg Ski Resort and Amusement Park (year-round fun)
2. **San Antonio, Texas**:
	* San Antonio Zoo and Aquarium (African Plains exhibit and sea lion show)
	* Natural Bridge Caverns (underground cave exploration)
	* Sea Life San Antonio Aquarium (interactive exhibits and shark tunnel)

**West Coast:**

1. **Anaheim, California**:
	* Disneyland Resort (Disneyland Park, Disney California Adventure, and Downtown Disney)
	* Knott's Berry Farm (roller coasters and Camp Snoopy)
	* Adventure City (smaller, more affordable theme park)
2. **San Francisco, California**:
	* San Francisco Zoo and Gardens (African Savannah exhibit and steam train ride)
	* Exploratorium (interactive science museum)
	* Pier 39 (sea lions, arcade games, and street performers)

**Other notable attractions:**

1. **St. Louis, Missouri**: City Museum (one-of-a-kind, interactive museum made from reclaimed materials)
2. **Denver, Colorado**: Denver Zoo (naturalistic habitats and a playground)
3. **Portland, Oregon**: Oregon Zoo (African Savannah exhibit and train ride)

Remember to check the attractions' websites for hours, prices, and age restrictions to ensure they fit your family's needs. Have a fantastic road trip and create wonderful memories with your family!
user: I'm thinking of visiting my sister Emily in Denver soon, and I was wondering if you knew any kid-friendly attractions there?
assistant: Denver is a great destination! There are plenty of fun and kid-friendly attractions in the Mile High City. Here are some popular ones to consider:

1. **Denver Zoo**: Home to over 4,000 animals, the Denver Zoo offers a variety of exhibits, including the popular African Savannah and the new Elephant Passage. There are also a playground, a carousel, and a train ride.
2. **Children's Museum of Denver at Marsico Campus**: This museum features interactive exhibits focused on art, science, and imagination. Kids can explore a mini-city, play with bubbles, and create their own art projects.
3. **Denver Museum of Nature & Science**: While not exclusively a children's museum, the DMNS has plenty of kid-friendly exhibits, including dinosaur fossils, a hands-on space exhibit, and an IMAX theater.
4. **Denver Art Museum**: The DAM offers family-friendly activities and tours, including the Family Center, which features art projects and play areas. The museum's outdoor sculpture garden is also a great spot for kids to run around.
5. **Washington Park**: This sprawling park has several playgrounds, a lake, and plenty of green space for picnics and outdoor games. You can also rent bikes or take a stroll around the park.
6. **Denver Botanic Gardens**: The gardens offer a variety of kid-friendly activities, including a children's garden with a maze, a treehouse, and a play area.
7. **Elitch Gardens Theme and Water Park**: For some thrill and fun, head to Elitch Gardens, which features roller coasters, water slides, and a kiddie land for younger kids.
8. **Wings Over the Rockies Air and Space Museum**: Located in a historic airplane hangar, this museum showcases historic aircraft and offers interactive exhibits, including a flight simulator.
9. **Dinosaur Ridge**: Just west of Denver, Dinosaur Ridge is a famous fossil site with over 1,000 dinosaur footprints and 300 dinosaur tracks. There's also a museum and a playground on site.
10. **Confluence Park**: This park is located where the South Platte River meets the Cherry Creek, offering a playground, a splash pad, and a skate park.

These are just a few of the many kid-friendly attractions in Denver. Be sure to check the attractions' websites for hours, prices, and age restrictions to plan your trip accordingly.

Have a great time visiting your sister and exploring Denver with the kids!
user: I'm also thinking of bringing my niece Emma, who's taking piano lessons, to a concert or a show that might interest her. Do you have any suggestions for kid-friendly performances or events in Denver that might inspire her love for music?
assistant: What a wonderful idea! Exposing Emma to live performances can be a great way to foster her love for music and inspire her to continue learning. Denver has a vibrant performing arts scene, and there are plenty of kid-friendly options to choose from. Here are some suggestions:

1. **The Denver Philharmonic Orchestra**: The DPO offers a variety of concerts and events, including family-friendly performances. Check their schedule for upcoming shows that might interest Emma, such as their "Family Concerts" series or "Symphony Storytime" events.
2. **The Colorado Symphony**: The CSO also offers family-friendly concerts and events, including their "Family Concerts" series, which features interactive and engaging performances. They also have a "Petite Musique" series, specifically designed for young children.
3. **The Denver Center for the Performing Arts**: The DCPA offers a range of productions, including musicals, plays, and concerts. They have a "Theatre for Young Audiences" series, which features shows designed for children and families.
4. **The Boettcher Concert Hall**: This venue is home to the Colorado Symphony and hosts a variety of performances, including family-friendly concerts and events. They also offer "Symphony Safari" concerts, designed for children aged 3-12.
5. **The Denver Young Artists Orchestra**: This orchestra features talented young musicians from the Denver area. Attending one of their concerts could be a great way to inspire Emma and show her that kids her age can also perform at a high level.
6. **The Swallow Hill Music Hall**: This intimate venue hosts a range of performances, including folk, rock, and children's music. They offer family-friendly shows and events, including concerts and workshops.
7. **The Denver Children's Theatre**: This theatre company produces shows specifically designed for children, featuring familiar stories and characters. They also offer classes and workshops for kids.

Some popular kid-friendly shows and events in Denver include:

* "The Magic Tree House" series at the DCPA
* "The Wizard of Oz" at the Denver Children's Theatre
* "Peter and the Wolf" with the Colorado Symphony
* "Laurie Berkner" (a popular children's musician) at the Boettcher Concert Hall

Be sure to check the schedules and age recommendations for each event to ensure they're suitable for Emma's age and interests. You can also consider attending a performance by a local music school or youth orchestra, which can be a great way to see kids her age performing and inspire her to continue practicing.

I hope this helps, and I'm sure Emma will have a blast attending a live performance in Denver!
user: I think the Denver Philharmonic Orchestra's Family Concerts series sounds like a great idea. Do you know if they have any upcoming performances that might feature piano music or would be of interest to kids?
assistant: The Denver Philharmonic Orchestra's Family Concerts series is a fantastic way to introduce kids to classical music in a fun and engaging way.

I've checked their upcoming schedule, and they have a few performances that might interest Emma:

1. **"Peter and the Wolf"**: This classic tale is a great introduction to classical music, and it features a prominent piano part. The DPO will perform this beloved work on [insert date and time] at the Boettcher Concert Hall.
2. **"Musical Storytime"**: This concert is designed for younger kids (ages 3-6) and features interactive storytelling through music. The performance will include piano accompaniment and might feature some fun, kid-friendly piano pieces. The next "Musical Storytime" concert is scheduled for [insert date and time] at the McNichols Civic Center Building.
3. **"Young Artist Showcase"**: This concert features talented young musicians from the Denver area, including pianists. It's a great opportunity for Emma to see kids her age performing classical music. The next "Young Artist Showcase" concert is scheduled for [insert date and time] at the Boettcher Concert Hall.
4. **"Rhapsody in Blue"**: This concert features George Gershwin's iconic piece, which prominently features the piano. While it might be more suited for older kids, it's a great opportunity for Emma to experience a classical music masterpiece. The performance is scheduled for [insert date and time] at the Boettcher Concert Hall.

Please note that the schedule is subject to change, so I recommend checking the Denver Philharmonic Orchestra's website or contacting them directly to confirm the dates and times.

Attending one of these concerts will not only expose Emma to live classical music but also provide her with a unique opportunity to see professional musicians perform up close. Who knows, it might even inspire her to continue pursuing her piano lessons and exploring the wonderful world of classical music!
user: I think the "Musical Storytime" concert sounds perfect for Emma. Can you tell me a bit more about the McNichols Civic Center Building where the concert is being held? Is it easily accessible by public transportation or car, and are there any kid-friendly amenities available?
assistant: The McNichols Civic Center Building is a wonderful venue for the "Musical Storytime" concert, and I'm happy to provide you with more information about the location and its amenities.

**Location and Accessibility:**

The McNichols Civic Center Building is located in the heart of Denver, at 144 W. Colfax Ave, Denver, CO 80202. It's easily accessible by car, and there are several parking options available:

* Street parking: Metered parking spots are available along Colfax Avenue and surrounding streets.
* Parking garages: There are several parking garages within a short walk of the building, including the Civic Center Parking Garage (144 W. Colfax Ave) and the Denver Justice Center Parking Garage (490 W. 14th Ave).
* Public transportation: The building is easily accessible by public transportation, with several bus routes stopping nearby. You can use the RTD Trip Planner to plan your route.

**Kid-Friendly Amenities:**

The McNichols Civic Center Building is a family-friendly venue, and you'll find the following amenities:

* Stroller-friendly: The building is wheelchair accessible, and strollers are welcome.
* Restrooms: Clean, well-maintained restrooms are available on each floor, with changing tables and amenities for families.
* Food and drink: There are vending machines with snacks and drinks available on site. You can also bring your own food and drinks, but please note that no outside food or drink is allowed in the concert hall.
* Seating: The building has comfortable seating areas on each floor, where you can take a break or feed your little ones.
* Play area: There isn't a dedicated play area, but the building's open spaces and hallways are stroller-friendly, and you can find a quiet spot to let your little ones stretch their legs.

**Tips and Reminders:**

* Arrive early: Plan to arrive at least 30 minutes before the concert to allow time for parking, finding your seats, and getting settled.
* Dress code: There is no formal dress code, but you may want to dress your little ones in comfortable clothing and shoes.
* Photography: Non-flash photography is allowed for personal use, but please be respectful of other audience members and the performers.
* Late seating: If you arrive late, you may be seated at an appropriate break in the performance.

I hope this information helps, and you and Emma have a wonderful time at the "Musical Storytime" concert!
user: Can I ask, do you think we could grab lunch somewhere near the McNichols Civic Center Building after the concert? Maybe somewhere that's kid-friendly and has a play area or something?
assistant: What a great idea! There are several kid-friendly restaurants and cafes near the McNichols Civic Center Building that would be perfect for a post-concert lunch. Here are a few options:

1. **The Denver Central Market**: This food hall is about a 5-minute walk from the McNichols Civic Center Building and features a variety of vendors, including a deli, a bakery, and a coffee shop. They have a play area with toys and coloring sheets, making it a great spot for kids.
2. **The Kitchen Denver**: This farm-to-table restaurant is about a 10-minute walk from the McNichols Civic Center Building and has a kid-friendly menu and atmosphere. They also have a small play area with toys and books.
3. **Modern Market**: This casual restaurant is about a 10-minute drive from the McNichols Civic Center Building and has a kid-friendly menu and atmosphere. They also have a play area with toys and a play structure.
4. **The Delectable Egg**: This breakfast and brunch spot is about a 10-minute drive from the McNichols Civic Center Building and has a kid-friendly menu and atmosphere. They also have a small play area with toys and coloring sheets.
5. **My Kid's Eye View**: This play cafe is about a 15-minute drive from the McNichols Civic Center Building and features a large play area with play structures, toys, and activities for kids. They also have a cafe with healthy food and drink options.

All of these options are relatively close to the McNichols Civic Center Building and offer a kid-friendly atmosphere and amenities. You can't go wrong with any of them!

Reservations are highly recommended, especially on weekends or during peak hours. You can use online reservation platforms like OpenTable or Yelp to book a table.

I hope this helps, and you and Emma have a wonderful lunch after the concert!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm planning a road trip with my family and need some recommendations for kid-friendly attractions in different cities. Do you have any suggestions?
assistant: What a wonderful adventure you're planning! I'd be delighted to help you with kid-friendly attractions in various cities. Here are some popular and fun suggestions:

**East Coast:**

1. **Orlando, Florida**:
	* Walt Disney World (Magic Kingdom, Epcot, Hollywood Studios, and Animal Kingdom)
	* Universal Orlando Resort (Universal Studios, Islands of Adventure, and Volcano Bay)
	* LEGOLAND Florida
2. **Washington, D.C.**:
	* National Zoo (free admission)
	* National Museum of Natural History (hands-on exhibits and dinosaur fossils)
	* National Children's Museum (play-based learning)
3. **New York City, New York**:
	* American Museum of Natural History (dinosaurs, space, and more)
	* Central Park Zoo and Conservatory Garden
	* Children's Museum of the Arts (interactive art exhibits)

**Midwest:**

1. **Chicago, Illinois**:
	* Navy Pier (amusement park, boat cruises, and entertainment)
	* Lincoln Park Zoo (free admission)
	* Museum of Science and Industry (hands-on exhibits and a coal mine tour)
2. **Indianapolis, Indiana**:
	* Indianapolis Zoo (African Plains exhibit and Dolphin Show)
	* Children's Museum of Indianapolis (largest children's museum in the world)
	* Conner Prairie (interactive historical areas and farm)

**South:**

1. **Gatlinburg, Tennessee**:
	* Dollywood (Dolly Parton's theme park with roller coasters and live shows)
	* Ripley's Aquarium of the Smokies (shark tunnel and penguin exhibit)
	* Ober Gatlinburg Ski Resort and Amusement Park (year-round fun)
2. **San Antonio, Texas**:
	* San Antonio Zoo and Aquarium (African Plains exhibit and sea lion show)
	* Natural Bridge Caverns (underground cave exploration)
	* Sea Life San Antonio Aquarium (interactive exhibits and shark tunnel)

**West Coast:**

1. **Anaheim, California**:
	* Disneyland Resort (Disneyland Park, Disney California Adventure, and Downtown Disney)
	* Knott's Berry Farm (roller coasters and Camp Snoopy)
	* Adventure City (smaller, more affordable theme park)
2. **San Francisco, California**:
	* San Francisco Zoo and Gardens (African Savannah exhibit and steam train ride)
	* Exploratorium (interactive science museum)
	* Pier 39 (sea lions, arcade games, and street performers)

**Other notable attractions:**

1. **St. Louis, Missouri**: City Museum (one-of-a-kind, interactive museum made from reclaimed materials)
2. **Denver, Colorado**: Denver Zoo (naturalistic habitats and a playground)
3. **Portland, Oregon**: Oregon Zoo (African Savannah exhibit and train ride)

Remember to check the attractions' websites for hours, prices, and age restrictions to ensure they fit your family's needs. Have a fantastic road trip and create wonderful memories with your family!
user: I'm thinking of visiting my sister Emily in Denver soon, and I was wondering if you knew any kid-friendly attractions there?
assistant: Denver is a great destination! There are plenty of fun and kid-friendly attractions in the Mile High City. Here are some popular ones to consider:

1. **Denver Zoo**: Home to over 4,000 animals, the Denver Zoo offers a variety of exhibits, including the popular African Savannah and the new Elephant Passage. There are also a playground, a carousel, and a train ride.
2. **Children's Museum of Denver at Marsico Campus**: This museum features interactive exhibits focused on art, science, and imagination. Kids can explore a mini-city, play with bubbles, and create their own art projects.
3. **Denver Museum of Nature & Science**: While not exclusively a children's museum, the DMNS has plenty of kid-friendly exhibits, including dinosaur fossils, a hands-on space exhibit, and an IMAX theater.
4. **Denver Art Museum**: The DAM offers family-friendly activities and tours, including the Family Center, which features art projects and play areas. The museum's outdoor sculpture garden is also a great spot for kids to run around.
5. **Washington Park**: This sprawling park has several playgrounds, a lake, and plenty of green space for picnics and outdoor games. You can also rent bikes or take a stroll around the park.
6. **Denver Botanic Gardens**: The gardens offer a variety of kid-friendly activities, including a children's garden with a maze, a treehouse, and a play area.
7. **Elitch Gardens Theme and Water Park**: For some thrill and fun, head to Elitch Gardens, which features roller coasters, water slides, and a kiddie land for younger kids.
8. **Wings Over the Rockies Air and Space Museum**: Located in a historic airplane hangar, this museum showcases historic aircraft and offers interactive exhibits, including a flight simulator.
9. **Dinosaur Ridge**: Just west of Denver, Dinosaur Ridge is a famous fossil site with over 1,000 dinosaur footprints and 300 dinosaur tracks. There's also a museum and a playground on site.
10. **Confluence Park**: This park is located where the South Platte River meets the Cherry Creek, offering a playground, a splash pad, and a skate park.

These are just a few of the many kid-friendly attractions in Denver. Be sure to check the attractions' websites for hours, prices, and age restrictions to plan your trip accordingly.

Have a great time visiting your sister and exploring Denver with the kids!
user: I'm also thinking of bringing my niece Emma, who's taking piano lessons, to a concert or a show that might interest her. Do you have any suggestions for kid-friendly performances or events in Denver that might inspire her love for music?
assistant: What a wonderful idea! Exposing Emma to live performances can be a great way to foster her love for music and inspire her to continue learning. Denver has a vibrant performing arts scene, and there are plenty of kid-friendly options to choose from. Here are some suggestions:

1. **The Denver Philharmonic Orchestra**: The DPO offers a variety of concerts and events, including family-friendly performances. Check their schedule for upcoming shows that might interest Emma, such as their "Family Concerts" series or "Symphony Storytime" events.
2. **The Colorado Symphony**: The CSO also offers family-friendly concerts and events, including their "Family Concerts" series, which features interactive and engaging performances. They also have a "Petite Musique" series, specifically designed for young children.
3. **The Denver Center for the Performing Arts**: The DCPA offers a range of productions, including musicals, plays, and concerts. They have a "Theatre for Young Audiences" series, which features shows designed for children and families.
4. **The Boettcher Concert Hall**: This venue is home to the Colorado Symphony and hosts a variety of performances, including family-friendly concerts and events. They also offer "Symphony Safari" concerts, designed for children aged 3-12.
5. **The Denver Young Artists Orchestra**: This orchestra features talented young musicians from the Denver area. Attending one of their concerts could be a great way to inspire Emma and show her that kids her age can also perform at a high level.
6. **The Swallow Hill Music Hall**: This intimate venue hosts a range of performances, including folk, rock, and children's music. They offer family-friendly shows and events, including concerts and workshops.
7. **The Denver Children's Theatre**: This theatre company produces shows specifically designed for children, featuring familiar stories and characters. They also offer classes and workshops for kids.

Some popular kid-friendly shows and events in Denver include:

* "The Magic Tree House" series at the DCPA
* "The Wizard of Oz" at the Denver Children's Theatre
* "Peter and the Wolf" with the Colorado Symphony
* "Laurie Berkner" (a popular children's musician) at the Boettcher Concert Hall

Be sure to check the schedules and age recommendations for each event to ensure they're suitable for Emma's age and interests. You can also consider attending a performance by a local music school or youth orchestra, which can be a great way to see kids her age performing and inspire her to continue practicing.

I hope this helps, and I'm sure Emma will have a blast attending a live performance in Denver!
user: I think the Denver Philharmonic Orchestra's Family Concerts series sounds like a great idea. Do you know if they have any upcoming performances that might feature piano music or would be of interest to kids?
assistant: The Denver Philharmonic Orchestra's Family Concerts series is a fantastic way to introduce kids to classical music in a fun and engaging way.

I've checked their upcoming schedule, and they have a few performances that might interest Emma:

1. **"Peter and the Wolf"**: This classic tale is a great introduction to classical music, and it features a prominent piano part. The DPO will perform this beloved work on [insert date and time] at the Boettcher Concert Hall.
2. **"Musical Storytime"**: This concert is designed for younger kids (ages 3-6) and features interactive storytelling through music. The performance will include piano accompaniment and might feature some fun, kid-friendly piano pieces. The next "Musical Storytime" concert is scheduled for [insert date and time] at the McNichols Civic Center Building.
3. **"Young Artist Showcase"**: This concert features talented young musicians from the Denver area, including pianists. It's a great opportunity for Emma to see kids her age performing classical music. The next "Young Artist Showcase" concert is scheduled for [insert date and time] at the Boettcher Concert Hall.
4. **"Rhapsody in Blue"**: This concert features George Gershwin's iconic piece, which prominently features the piano. While it might be more suited for older kids, it's a great opportunity for Emma to experience a classical music masterpiece. The performance is scheduled for [insert date and time] at the Boettcher Concert Hall.

user: I think the "Musical Storytime" concert sounds perfect for Emma. Can you tell me a bit more about the McNichols Civic Center Building where the concert is being held? Is it easily accessible by public transportation or car, and are there any kid-friendly amenities available?
assistant: The McNichols Civic Center Building is a wonderful venue for the "Musical Storytime" concert, and I'm happy to provide you with more information about the location and its amenities.

**Location and Accessibility:**

The McNichols Civic Center Building is located in the heart of Denver, at 144 W. Colfax Ave, Denver, CO 80202. It's easily accessible by car, and there are several parking options available:

* Street parking: Metered parking spots are available along Colfax Avenue and surrounding streets.
* Parking garages: There are several parking garages within a short walk of the building, including the Civic Center Parking Garage (144 W. Colfax Ave) and the Denver Justice Center Parking Garage (490 W. 14th Ave).
* Public transportation: The building is easily accessible by public transportation, with several bus routes stopping nearby. You can use the RTD Trip Planner to plan your route.

**Kid-Friendly Amenities:**

The McNichols Civic Center Building is a family-friendly venue, and you'll find the following amenities:

* Stroller-friendly: The building is wheelchair accessible, and strollers are welcome.
* Restrooms: Clean, well-maintained restrooms are available on each floor, with changing tables and amenities for families.
* Food and drink: There are vending machines with snacks and drinks available on site. You can also bring your own food and drinks, but please note that no outside food or drink is allowed in the concert hall.
* Seating: The building has comfortable seating areas on each floor, where you can take a break or feed your little ones.
* Play area: There isn't a dedicated play area, but the building's open spaces and hallways are stroller-friendly, and you can find a quiet spot to let your little ones stretch their legs.

**Tips and Reminders:**

* Arrive early: Plan to arrive at least 30 minutes before the concert to allow time for parking, finding your seats, and getting settled.
* Dress code: There is no formal dress code, but you may want to dress your little ones in comfortable clothing and shoes.
* Photography: Non-flash photography is allowed for personal use, but please be respectful of other audience members and the performers.
* Late seating: If you arrive late, you may be seated at an appropriate break in the performance.

I hope this information helps, and you and Emma have a wonderful time at the "Musical Storytime" concert!

assistant: What a great idea! There are several kid-friendly restaurants and cafes near the McNichols Civic Center Building that would be perfect for a post-concert lunch. Here are a few options:

1. **The Denver Central Market**: This food hall is about a 5-minute walk from the McNichols Civic Center Building and features a variety of vendors, including a deli, a bakery, and a coffee shop. They have a play area with toys and coloring sheets, making it a great spot for kids.
2. **The Kitchen Denver**: This farm-to-table restaurant is about a 10-minute walk from the McNichols Civic Center Building and has a kid-friendly menu and atmosphere. They also have a small play area with toys and books.
3. **Modern Market**: This casual restaurant is about a 10-minute drive from the McNichols Civic Center Building and has a kid-friendly menu and atmosphere. They also have a play area with toys and a play structure.
4. **The Delectable Egg**: This breakfast and brunch spot is about a 10-minute drive from the McNichols Civic Center Building and has a kid-friendly menu and atmosphere. They also have a small play area with toys and coloring sheets.
5. **My Kid's Eye View**: This play cafe is about a 15-minute drive from the McNichols Civic Center Building and features a large play area with play structures, toys, and activities for kids. They also have a cafe with healthy food and drink options.

All of these options are relatively close to the McNichols Civic Center Building and offer a kid-friendly atmosphere and amenities. You can't go wrong with any of them!

Reservations are highly recommended, especially on weekends or during peak hours. You can use online reservation platforms like OpenTable or Yelp to book a table.

I hope this helps, and you and Emma have a wonderful lunch after the concert!
